plan.description: The project focuses on the role of the Parkinson´s Disease associated protein alpha-synuclein and how it impacts the dynamics and activity of the proteasome. The project will make use of human embryonic stem cells which will be differentiated into neuronal and glial cells for further analysis. The majority of the data generated within the project will be microscopy data, as well as immunohbydirization data and sequencing data.
plan.title: WBP- Tariq Tammam Ali

blueprint.label: EUREMAP Plan Blueprint

systemField.label: Title of Plan
systemField.description:
The tile of your DMP Plan should follow the format:

EUREMAP Data Management Plan - [Project leader's name]

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the project and the data that will be created in general terms.

systemField.label: Contact
systemField.description: Name of contact person for this DMP
What access rights - licence - will you give your data. EUREMAP recommends CC BY

extraField.label: Hosting Institution
extraField.description: The hosting institute: the institute where the work will be conducted

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Provide the names and ORCID IDs of the persons who will create, process, or analyse the data described in this DMP. If they do not appear in the drop down, you can add them manually.

extraField.label: Introduction
extraField.description:
Ethical considerations in science become crucial whenever your research involves potential harm to living organisms, ecosystems, or communities. If your research fits into these criteria please answer following questions. As a default please fill the Horizon Europe - Ethics template (it only has 2 questions), however other templates are available.
You do not need to write anything in the box here; use those in the data description template you select.

extraField.label: Introduction
extraField.description:
Please select a template to describe your dataset that best fits your research topic. You can chose more than one template. All the templates prepared for the EUREMAP project are called "EUREMAP | category | datatype". If you can't find a data-specific template that fits your research, please use the "EUREMAP | Generic | Template".
You do not need to write anything in the box here; use those in the template you select.

blueprint.description:
This is the EUREMAP Data Management Plan (DMP) designed to ensure compliance with Horizon Europe mandates on Open Science, including Open Access publication and Research Data Management.

The DMP contains common questions about your project and the data you will create. Each data set (or data type if multiple data sets of the same type are created) should be described by adding a "description templates" for that data type to the plan. Multiple different "description templates" for each of you data types can be added to this plan. For example, if you generate genomics data, use the genomics "description template" (if available); if you generate LC-MS data, use the LC-MS description template (if available). The generic template "EUREMAP template" can be used for all types of data.

blueprintValue.value: The impact of α-Synuclein on proteasome activity: Paving the way for targeted protein degradation

description.description: Highlight all relevant points as in regards to ethical considerations
description.label: Horizon Europe - Ethics

descriptionTemplate.label: Horizon Europe - Ethics
descriptionTemplate.description: A template of Horizon Europe about ethics.

fieldSet.title: Are there, or could there be, any ethics or legal issues that can have an impact on data sharing? These can also be discussed in the context of the ethics review. If relevant, include references to ethics deliverables and ethics chapter in the Description of the Action (DoA).
Will informed consent for data sharing and long term preservation be included in questionnaires dealing with personal data?

field.textValue: The project involves the use of human embryonic stem cells as well as post-mortem brain samples. As such GDPR principles must be adhered to. All data published and shared will be anonymized.

Embryonic stem cells and post-mortem tissue have already been donated. Consent was given for use of materials in research. Not further contact with donors or next-of-kin is planned and as such further questionaires are not required.

descriptionTemplate.label: EUREMAP | generic | template
descriptionTemplate.description: A generic data set description template for the EUREMAP project

fieldSet.title: Data Types
fieldSet.description:
What type(s) of data will you create or re-use in your project. What is(are) their format(s)?

Examples
  • I will be collecting DNA sequences (FASTQ) and creating bioinformatics outputs (various text files)
  • I will be collecting assay measurements from my experiment in a spreadsheet (CSV)

fieldSet.title: Data Creation
fieldSet.description:
How will/were the data types/data sets described in 1.1.1. created?

Examples
  • The data will be the result of field collections
  • The data will be the output of the following: a FlowCam, Fluorescence microscopy,  etc...
  • The data will be the collected measurements made from my laboratory experiments

fieldSet.title: Published Data Re-use
fieldSet.description:
Will you re-use any previously published data? 
If Yes: state why you are using the data, and where the data originate from

Examples
  • I will include use existing assay data from EU-OPENSCREEN ECDB database
  • I will use existing DNA sequence data from EMBL ENA in my molecular phylogenetic analysis

fieldSet.title: Data size
fieldSet.description: What is the expected size(s) (in GBs) of the data set(s) that you intend to create and/or re-use? If you are unsure, make a guess.

section.description:
This is the Generic Data Type Description Template. If a data type specific EUREMAP template exists, you should use that instead. Please search for other description templates using the word "EUREMAP".

Ideally, each data type should have a a separate description template. So one for spreadsheet recorded values, a separate one for sequence data, and a third for assay results, for instance.

What data type does this template describe that will be created or re-used in your project?
section.title: Types of data and usage

fieldSet.title: Data Publication Repository
fieldSet.description:
In which repository, database, or data catalogue will you publish your data set(s)? Please indicate a publication venue for each of your data types or data sets, if you will publish in more than one place. 

Recommedations:

If you are doing organismal experimentation, use the following services/repositories:

  • BioStudies - for study metadata and result data in tabular format, with some scope to include images (depending on size). We suggest all organismal studies should include a BioStudies accession to record project metadata, and possibly to deposit result data
  •  BioSamples - to register organismal samples in the BioStudies accession and add sample specific metadata 
  •  EMBL ENA - for sequence data, incl. metagenomics, transciptomics, etc
  • ArrayExpress - microarray or qPCR data
  • ChEMBL - molecular assay results, structures, etc…
  • Metabolights - for spectrographic data (LC-MS, UV-vis, etc)
  • Protocols.IO - for protocols if these are an output
Only if there is not a more data-specific repository you could use should you use Zenodo or Figshare, or some other (unstructured) repository - this is likely to be a very rare occurance.

You may be required to archive your data in a repository of your university/organisation. If that repository is not accessible to everyone, then you should also publish your data in one of these other mentioned repositories. But even if that is open to everyone, you should publish in one of these other mentioned repositories as your data are more globally findable in those.

fieldSet.title: Metadata collection
fieldSet.description:
How will you collect your dataset description and provenance metadata? 

Reminder: metadata are the descriptive data that contextualise and describe the actual data themselves and make your data findable, re-usable, and reproducible. This includes information that describe these aspects:
  • WHO - did the work
  • WHEN - was the work done
  • WHERE - was the work conducted, the field work/sampling done
  • WHAT - parameters and samples (use BioSamples accessions if appropriate) did you collect/work with
  • WHAT - instruments, software, platforms wer used
  • WHAT - what data files are in your data set and what do they contain 
  • HOW - were the sampling, sample processing, experiments, and software analysis done (register methods and protocols with protocols.io)
If you create a BioStudies accession for your project, then your data can be included in that accession and linked to any other data you publish in other repositories.

Examples
WHO, WHEN, and WHERE
I will collect the following information in a spreadsheet: the names and ORCID IDs of all the people involved in the work, the coordinates of my sampling field work, the name of service provider where I did the work, the dates of the sample collection and the subsequent sample and data processing.
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies. 

WHAT
I will ask the service provider for the types/names and IDs of all the instruments and devices I will use.
My samples will be given an ID that I will create, and these IDs will be included in my experimental data and be linked to all photographs I take of the specimens. 
I will record the name and version of the software that I used (it will be my own software that I will subsequently make available via GitHub).
I will create a README to describe each file in my data set. 
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies.

HOW
I will also ask the service provider for the URL/DOI of their protocols that I followed and will record this in my README. If those protocols are not published, I will instead ask for a PDF copy and I will include this as part of my dataset. 
I will publish my own protocol on the protocols.io EUREMAP Community. I will then add all the DOI to my README.
I will only use my own software, and the settings will be included within the code I will publish. 
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies.

section.description: How you will make your data and metadata findable (N.B. metadata are the data that describe and contextualise the actual data that you are collecting in your project, and make them understandable to everyone else.)
section.title: Findable

section.description: How the data will be made accessible.
section.title: Accessible

fieldSet.title: Open Access Data Repositories
fieldSet.description:
Are the repositories you indicated in 2.1.1 for publication of data and metadata Open Access (i.e. can anyone see the data and metadata, or is access limited to certain people)?

Your answer should be YES. If not explain, why.

fieldSet.title: Persistent URLs and identifiers
fieldSet.description:
Do the repositories you indicated in 2.1.1 ensure that the data is assigned a long-term URL (html link that takes you to the metadata or the data) or a DOI?

Your answer should be YES. If not, explain why.

fieldSet.title: Licence
fieldSet.description:
What open licence will you publish your data under? 

Your answer should be CC-BY 4.0. If not, explain why.

fieldSet.title: Data Publication Embargo
fieldSet.description:
Will you apply an data publication embargo?

All data created by your study should be uploaded to the appropriate repository as soon as possible after its collection. This is to ensure that data are deposited when they are most memorable and are not lost to time - unfortunately a great deal of data that are collected are never published. After data deposition to a repository, an embargo on its public release from the repository can be enacted.

fieldSet.title: Restricted Data Usage
fieldSet.description:
Are there are restrictions on re-use of the data you will create?  For example, due to Intellectual Property rights, sensitive data (esp. if human data). 

If YES: how will access be provided to the data, both during and after the end of the project?

Example 
  • I am collecting data about ecologically sensitive species, and so will keep some locality information (specific locations for example) private

fieldSet.title: Unpublished Data
fieldSet.description:
Are there any data you create that you cannot, or will not, publish publicly?

Note that null results and incidental data should be published - i.e. negative results and/or data that is not useful to your science (hence will not be published in scientific journal) should nevertheless be published in an Open Access repository.

Your answer should be NO. 
if YES: please explain why

fieldSet.title: Open Data Formats
fieldSet.description:
Are the formats in which you publish your data open?

If NO: please list the non-open formats and explain why you have to use them.

A closed format needs a proprietary software application to be read (i.e. typical examples are spreadsheets in MS Excel format xlsx. Some instruments (e.g. LC-MS) also produce data that can only be read by proprietary (commercial) instrument-specific software - these data should be converted to open formats before publication. 
An open format can be opened and read by anyone without needing specific software to do that. Examples are: CSV, TSV, FASTA, FASTQ, PDF, JPG
If in doubt: try wikipedia! 

NB some formats are not open but are openable with free software: for example, MP4 and XLSX. If you can convert these to open formats (e.g. CSV instead of XLSX), please do so.  

Examples
  • The instruments I will use produce data in proprietary formats: I will convert these to open formats before publication.
  • I will convert my MS Excel files to CSV before I publish them, so they are in an open format

fieldSet.title: Vocabularies and Ontologies
fieldSet.description:
Will you use controlled vocabularies or ontologies in your data and metadata?

Controlled vocabularies contain terms that are agreed by scientific communities working within the field of expertise. These terms are written in a controlled way and have one definition that everyone has agreed on. The terms can be found on the web via a URL (web address), and will have an ID by which they can be referred to as well as the actual term name (a string). 
Ontologies are glorified vocabularies, as they additionally ascribe meaning and relations between terms of a vocabulary. 

Examples are:
  • MIXS - Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence (MIxS) standard
  • ENVO - The Environment Ontology
  • EDAM - Ontology of bioscientific data analysis and data management
  • EFO - Experimental Factor Ontology
  • NCBI Taxonomy Database - Classification and nomenclature for all organisms

Examples
  • I will use terms from ENVO to describe the habitats relevant to my experiment. I will use their recommended URLs for the terms (http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ENVO_12345678)
  • I will use NVS to describe measured parameters and their units. I will use their recommended URLs for the terms  (http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P01/current/ABCDEDG/)
  • I will use MiXS when uploading my data to ENA, as this is mandatory
  • I will use NCBI taxonomy for all taxon names, and will use their recommended IDs for the names (NCBI:txid123456)

section.description: How will you make the data you publish interoperable with other data (i.e. how will you standardise your data)?
section.title: Interoperable

fieldSet.title: Methods and Protocols
fieldSet.description:
Will all your methods, protocols, operating procedures, computer scripts, software execution parameters (etc) be published  as Open Access?

If YES: add an explanation of where and how: we recommend the EUREMAP Community on Protocols.IO
If NO: please explain why

Example
  • I will publish my methods in protocols.io and my analyses scripts and software will be stored on GitHub and published via Zenodo.

section.description: How will the data publication enhance data re-use.
section.title: Re-useable (and reproducible)

field.textValue: No previously published data will be reused in the project.
Data will be publishes under the CC-BY 4.0 license.

At the onset of the project I estimate:
  1. 5-6 GB worth of microscopy data to be generated.
  2. ~1 GB worth of Western Blot Data
  3. <100 MB of Sequencing Data
  4. < 50 MB of Quantification Data

Protocolls will be published alongside the peer reviewed article. We aim to publish our articles in open access journals. Should this not be the case protocols will be made readily available through the online repositories Protocols.io

1. The project wil collect a large amount of microscopy images, which will be saved as TIFF files. In addition copies of the images will be saved in the proprietary software connected to the microscopes.
  2. Moreover, the project will include a variety of Western Blot data, which will be saved in TIFF format as well as in the proprietary data format of the imaging machine.
  3. Sequencing data will be stored as FASTA files.
  4. Quantifications will be saved in CSV or alternatively FAIR compliant file formats such as GraphPAd Prism.
Sensitive donor-related data from post-mortem brain tissues can be not be made publically. Information relevant to research however will be accessible.
Neither specific Vocabularies or Ontologies will be used.

1. Microscopy images are the result of matured cells, which are derived from human embryonic stem cells or immunohistochemically stained patient tissue. 
  2. Western Blot data dervies from proteins extracts generated from differentiated cells derived from human embryonic stem cells.
  3. Sequencing data will be generated by an external service providers, using samples taken from bacteria as well as human cells, which have been derived from human embryonic stem cells. 
  4. Quantifications are the result of analysis of microscopy images, activity assays and Western blot Quantifications.

Generated data will not include Metabolomics, Proteomics or Genomics and does not include data gathered from organismal experiments. Generated data will be deposited within the Host Institution and will be made widely avaiable through Zenodo.
Protocols will be published as part of peer-reviewd articles, however, should those not be open access the protocols will be made publically available through protocols.io
All data published will be published in an open format. Internal copies of original data in proprietary data formats will not be shared publically.
Data will embargoed for public use until the related publications have been published in a peer-reviewed journal.
Yes all repositories will be open access.
Each project and the associated data will  be given a DOI

The data management plan involving hESC differentiation experiments, metadata collection and organization will follow the CERIF standard to ensure interoperability and FAIR compliance. 
  • Who (cfPerson, cfOrgUnit): Record principal investigators, lab personnel, and institutional affiliations with ORCID IDs, roles, and contact details.
  • Where (cfGeoLocation): Document lab location, equipment used, and sample storage sites with geographic and infrastructure metadata. 
  • When (cfTime): Timestamp all stages—cell line derivation, differentiation protocols, imaging dates—with creation, modification, and validity periods. 
  • How (cfService, cfEquipment, cfProj): Detail protocols, reagents, software, and methods using semantic classifications and links to projects. 
  • What (cfResPubl, cfResProd): Catalog datasets, analyses, and outputs with multilingual abstracts, DOIs, keywords, and measurements like citation metrics. Data will be exported as CERIF XML for repository deposition with referential integrity via link entities
